title: Merck — RSV — Knowledge Doc
html:
Enflonsia (clesrovimab) -- RSV Infant Prevention

Governing Thought

Enflonsia's competitive viability against Beyfortus hinges not on clinical superiority (Beyfortus has stronger efficacy data and massive real-world evidence) but on operational differentiation -- simplified dosing, superior storage, and reliable supply -- that can convert practice-level convenience into formulary access during the narrow seasonal ordering window. The battle is won or lost at the pediatrician's purchasing decision, not the clinical data table.

1. Market Landscape

The Only Question That Matters

Can Enflonsia take meaningful share from Beyfortus, which has first-mover advantage, 6M+ infants immunized, and 40+ real-world studies?

Market Structure

The infant RSV prevention market is bifurcated:

Segment Products Mechanism Status
Passive immunization (infant) Beyfortus (Sanofi/AZ), Enflonsia (Merck) Long-acting mAb, single IM dose Beyfortus launched 2023; Enflonsia launched July 2025
Maternal vaccination Abrysvo (Pfizer) Protein subunit vaccine for pregnant women (32-36 weeks) Low uptake (~18% in 2023-24 season)
Legacy (discontinued) Synagis (Sobi) Monthly mAb, high-risk only Discontinued Dec 31, 2025

Market Size

  • Global RSV vaccines market: $3.51B (2024), projected $28.4B by 2030 (CAGR 42%)
  • Infant/children segment: 76% of market (2024)
  • Beyfortus 2025 revenue: EUR 1.78B (~$2.1B), +9.5% YoY
  • Passive immunization: 75% of RSV market revenue share (2024)

RSV Seasonality

  • US continental season: October through March (most regions)
  • Peak ordering/stocking decisions: July-September (pre-season)
  • Tropical US areas (southern FL, HI, PR, Guam): different patterns
  • Alaska: less predictable, longer seasons

2. Product Profiles

Enflonsia (clesrovimab-cfor) -- Merck

Attribute Detail
Mechanism Long-acting mAb targeting conserved site on RSV F protein (pre- and post-fusion forms)
FDA Approval June 9, 2025
ACIP Recommendation June 26, 2025 -- no preferential recommendation between nirsevimab and clesrovimab
Indication Prevention of RSV LRTD in neonates/infants born during or entering first RSV season
Dose Single 105 mg IM injection -- same dose regardless of weight
Duration of protection 5 months (typical RSV season)
Efficacy 60.5% reduction in RSV-associated MA-LRTI; 84.3% reduction in RSV hospitalizations
Safety Generally comparable to placebo; >=97% of adverse reactions grade 1-2
Storage 30-month shelf life; up to 48 hours at room temperature
Price $556/dose
Commercial launch July 2025, ahead of 2025-26 RSV season
VFC availability As early as October 2, 2025
Insurance coverage ~93% of commercially insured pediatric patients covered (as of Oct 2025)

Key Trial: CANOPY (MK-1654-007)

  • Phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled
  • Healthy infants born during or entering first RSV season
  • Primary endpoint: RSV-associated MA-LRTI through 150 days

Label Expansion: SMART Trial (Phase 3)

  • Feb 2026: Positive data for second-season use in children <2 years at increased risk
  • Achieved serum concentrations similar to healthy infants in pivotal trial
  • Filing planned with FDA and other regulatory authorities
  • If approved, would expand to match Beyfortus second-season indication

Beyfortus (nirsevimab-alip) -- Sanofi/AstraZeneca

Attribute Detail
Mechanism Long-acting mAb targeting RSV F protein
FDA Approval July 2023
Indication Prevention of RSV LRTD in neonates/infants entering first RSV season + children <24 months at increased risk through second RSV season
Dose Weight-based: 50 mg (<5 kg) or 100 mg (>=5 kg)
Duration 5 months (US label); 6 months (EU label update)
Efficacy 74.9% reduction MA-LRTI (healthy term/late preterm); 70.1% (healthy preterm)
Real-world evidence 40+ studies, 250,000+ infants; 6M+ infants immunized globally
Supply Tripled production capacity, doubled manufacturing sites since 2023 launch
Price $556/dose (matched pricing)
2025 revenue EUR 1.78B (~$2.1B)

Supply History:

  • 2023-24 season: Severe supply shortage within 1.5 months of launch -- demand exceeded aggressive supply plan
  • 2024-25 season: Reservation program introduced; supply largely stabilized
  • 2025-26 season: No supply challenges anticipated; supply already matches prior year total distribution

Abrysvo (Pfizer) -- Maternal Vaccine

Attribute Detail
Type Protein subunit vaccine for pregnant women
Timing Weeks 32-36 of pregnancy, September-January
Uptake Low: ~18% overall (2023-24 season); highest among Asian (25%), lowest among Black (10%)
Coverage improvement 2024-25 season: RSV immunization (maternal + nirsevimab) reached ~57% nationally
Challenge Parental vaccine hesitancy, narrow administration window, obstetric workflow integration

3. Competitive Positioning -- Head-to-Head

Where Enflonsia Wins

  1. Dosing simplicity -- Single 105 mg dose regardless of weight (Beyfortus requires weight-based 50/100 mg)
  2. Storage/handling -- 30-month shelf life + 48-hour room temperature stability
  3. Operational convenience -- No weight calculation, fewer stocking SKUs, simpler ordering
  4. Supply reliability -- Merck has adequate supply; no history of shortages (vs. Beyfortus 2023 debacle)

Where Beyfortus Wins

  1. Efficacy data -- 74.9% MA-LRTI reduction vs. Enflonsia's 60.5% (not head-to-head, but directionally clear)
  2. Real-world evidence -- 40+ studies, 6M+ infants (Enflonsia has zero RWE)
  3. First-mover advantage -- 2 years of commercial experience, established in practice workflows
  4. Second-season indication -- Already approved for children <24 months at high risk (Enflonsia filing pending)
  5. Duration claim -- EU label: 6 months vs. Enflonsia's 5 months
  6. HCP familiarity/comfort -- Top barrier to Enflonsia stocking: "committed to/currently using Beyfortus"

Parity

  • Price: Both $556/dose
  • Both ACIP-recommended with no preferential recommendation
  • Both VFC-eligible
  • Both single-dose, single-visit administration
  • Both 5-month protection on US label

4. ZoomRx Research Assets

PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking)

  • Design: Mobile/web survey with voice response, ~15 min, fielded Q4 '25 and Q1 '26
  • Sample: ~64 Enflonsia interactions, ~63 Beyfortus interactions per wave
  • Target: Pediatric and Family Practice MDs, Seg A & B HCPs, 25+ patients 0-24 months/month
  • Measures: Promotional reach, share of voice, message recall/effectiveness, rep performance, HCP perceptions, stocking likelihood

Digital Tracker

  • Design: Passive tracking of HCP web browsing, paid search, emails, banner ads
  • Panel: ~121-125 active HCPs on Enflonsia target list
  • Channels tracked: Web browsing, paid search, emails, banner ads
  • Timeframes: Q3 '25 (Aug-Oct '25) and Q1 '26 (Jan-Mar '26)

5. Key HCP Insights (from PET Q1 '26)

Promotional Activity

  • Beyfortus reach significantly higher: 80% of MDs reached by Beyfortus PP vs. 50% by Enflonsia PP
  • Beyfortus frequency higher: 1.3 visits/HCP vs. 1.1 for Enflonsia
  • NPP gap even wider: 95% Beyfortus NPP reach vs. 41% Enflonsia
  • Enflonsia SoV growing: Share of voice increased directionally Q4->Q1

Message Effectiveness

Enflonsia top messages (by ME score):

  1. ENF2 -- Same dose regardless of weight (79% ME, 84% recall) -- STRONGEST
  2. ENF10 -- Single 105 mg IM injection (73% ME, 69% recall)
  3. ENF8 -- 30-month shelf life (68% ME, 63% recall)

Beyfortus top messages (by ME score):

  1. BEYF1 -- First long-acting antibody indication (72% ME)
  2. BEYF12 -- 6M+ infants immunized, real-world data (67% ME)
  3. BEYF3 -- 74.9% efficacy reduction (67% ME)

HCP Product Perceptions (post-rep interaction)

  • Enflonsia significantly higher: Dosing & administration, shelf-life, hours out of cold storage, operational benefits
  • Beyfortus significantly higher: Efficacy, safety
  • Comparable: Overall impression, length of protection, cost/coverage

Stocking Intent

  • Only ~33% of HCPs report high likelihood to stock Enflonsia for current season
  • Beyfortus stocking likelihood significantly higher
  • Top barriers to Enflonsia stocking:
    1. Committed to/currently using Beyfortus (declining Q4->Q1: -26%)
    2. Financial uncertainty (cost, pricing, reimbursement) (+8%)
    3. Practice policy: only stock one RSV product (+10%)
    4. Too late in RSV season to change products
  • For next season (Oct '26-Mar '27): "Happy with/unwilling to change from Beyfortus" remains top barrier

Rep Performance

  • Enflonsia reps trail Beyfortus reps across most performance metrics
  • Key gaps: Addressing questions, providing credible support, compelling reason to recommend
  • Enflonsia reps close >90% of interactions (comparable to Beyfortus)
  • Trust and best-in-class perception declined Q4->Q1 for Enflonsia reps

Digital Landscape

  • RSV browsing = ~2% of total healthcare browsing among target HCPs
  • Beyfortus dominates brand-related browsing time
  • Enflonsia gained paid search share: 22% on general RSV terms (vs. 1% Beyfortus) and 27% on Beyfortus keyword terms
  • Email reach comparable between brands; Beyfortus emails rated more relevant
  • Banner ad exposure sparse: 94% of HCPs saw no market-basket RSV ads

CDC Schedule Change Impact (Jan 2026)

  • 65% of HCPs aware of changes
  • ~70% say recommendation will not change; ~15% will recommend more
  • 2/3 of HCPs expect number of infants receiving RSV prevention will decline
  • Decline driver: increased parent refusal, not reduced HCP advocacy
  • HCPs relying more on AAP/AAFP than CDC/ACIP for guidance

6. Strategic Implications for Merck

What's Working

  1. Dosing message resonance -- ENF2 (same dose regardless of weight) is the highest-recall, highest-effectiveness message in the Enflonsia portfolio. This is Enflonsia's identity.
  2. Operational differentiation -- Storage, shelf life, and dosing simplicity create real practice-level value that HCPs recognize
  3. Paid search expansion -- Successfully bidding on general RSV and Beyfortus keywords
  4. Insurance coverage breadth -- 93% commercial coverage removes a key barrier
  5. Supply positioning -- "Adequate supply" messaging resonates given Beyfortus history

What Needs Fixing

  1. Reach gap -- Beyfortus reaches 60% more HCPs via PP and 130% more via NPP. This is the single biggest structural disadvantage.
  2. Efficacy perception -- HCPs associate efficacy and duration messages more with Beyfortus. Enflonsia needs RWE urgently.
  3. Rep capability -- Enflonsia reps trail across knowledge, credibility, and question-handling metrics. Training investment needed.
  4. Mid-season switching inertia -- Most HCPs won't switch mid-season. Pre-season (July-Sept) is the only window that matters.
  5. Second-season indication gap -- Beyfortus has it; Enflonsia is filing. This matters for high-risk infant coverage.
  6. Cost/access conversations -- HCPs want more clarity on coverage; reps significantly underdeliver on this topic vs. Beyfortus

Strategic Priorities

  1. Win the pre-season window (July-September) with dosing + storage + supply message combination
  2. Close the RWE gap -- Include any available real-world data in materials ASAP
  3. Fix rep performance -- Specifically on addressing HCP questions and providing credible support
  4. Prioritize cost/access conversations -- HCPs are asking; reps aren't delivering
  5. Secure second-season filing -- SMART trial data supports it; filing timing is critical
  6. Target Beyfortus-frustrated practices -- Those who experienced supply issues or dislike weight-based dosing

7. Key Dates and Milestones

Date Event
Jul 2023 Beyfortus FDA approval
Oct-Dec 2023 Beyfortus supply shortage
Jun 9, 2025 Enflonsia FDA approval
Jun 26, 2025 ACIP recommends Enflonsia (no preferential rec)
Jul 2025 Enflonsia commercial launch / ordering begins
Oct 2, 2025 Enflonsia available on VFC contract
Oct 2025 93% commercial insurance coverage achieved
Dec 31, 2025 Synagis discontinued
Jan 2026 CDC childhood immunization schedule changes
Feb 2026 SMART trial positive second-season data announced
2026 (TBD) Enflonsia second-season indication filing with FDA
Oct 2026 2026-27 RSV season begins -- first full competitive season

Channels tracked: Web browsing, paid search, emails, banner ads
Timeframes: Q3 '25 (Aug-Oct '25) and Q1 '26 (Jan-Mar '26)

Promotional Activity

Beyfortus frequency higher: 1.3 visits/HCP vs. 1.1 for Enflonsia
NPP gap even wider: 95% Beyfortus NPP reach vs. 41% Enflonsia
Enflonsia SoV growing: Share of voice increased directionally Q4->Q1

Message Effectiveness

ENF2 -- Same dose regardless of weight (79% ME, 84% recall) -- STRONGEST
ENF10 -- Single 105 mg IM injection (73% ME, 69% recall)
ENF8 -- 30-month shelf life (68% ME, 63% recall)

Beyfortus top messages (by ME score):

BEYF1 -- First long-acting antibody indication (72% ME)
BEYF12 -- 6M+ infants immunized, real-world data (67% ME)
BEYF3 -- 74.9% efficacy reduction (67% ME)

HCP Product Perceptions (post-rep interaction)

Enflonsia significantly higher: Dosing & administration, shelf-life, hours out of cold storage, operational benefits
Beyfortus significantly higher: Efficacy, safety
Comparable: Overall impression, length of protection, cost/coverage

Stocking Intent

Only ~33% of HCPs report high likelihood to stock Enflonsia for current season
Beyfortus stocking likelihood significantly higher
Top barriers to Enflonsia stocking:

Committed to/currently using Beyfortus (declining Q4->Q1: -26%)
Financial uncertainty (cost, pricing, reimbursement) (+8%)
Practice policy: only stock one RSV product (+10%)
Too late in RSV season to change products


For next season (Oct '26-Mar '27): "Happy with/unwilling to change from Beyfortus" remains top barrier

Rep Performance

Enflonsia reps trail Beyfortus reps across most performance metrics
Key gaps: Addressing questions, providing credible support, compelling reason to recommend
Enflonsia reps close >90% of interactions (comparable to Beyfortus)
Trust and best-in-class perception declined Q4->Q1 for Enflonsia reps

Digital Landscape

RSV browsing = ~2% of total healthcare browsing among target HCPs
Beyfortus dominates brand-related browsing time
Enflonsia gained paid search share: 22% on general RSV terms (vs. 1% Beyfortus) and 27% on Beyfortus keyword terms
Email reach comparable between brands; Beyfortus emails rated more relevant
Banner ad exposure sparse: 94% of HCPs saw no market-basket RSV ads

CDC Schedule Change Impact (Jan 2026)

65% of HCPs aware of changes
~70% say recommendation will not change; ~15% will recommend more
2/3 of HCPs expect number of infants receiving RSV prevention will decline
Decline driver: increased parent refusal, not reduced HCP advocacy
HCPs relying more on AAP/AAFP than CDC/ACIP for guidance

What's Working

Dosing message resonance -- ENF2 (same dose regardless of weight) is the highest-recall, highest-effectiveness message in the Enflonsia portfolio. This is Enflonsia's identity.
Operational differentiation -- Storage, shelf life, and dosing simplicity create real practice-level value that HCPs recognize
Paid search expansion -- Successfully bidding on general RSV and Beyfortus keywords
Insurance coverage breadth -- 93% commercial coverage removes a key barrier
Supply positioning -- "Adequate supply" messaging resonates given Beyfortus history

What Needs Fixing

Reach gap -- Beyfortus reaches 60% more HCPs via PP and 130% more via NPP. This is the single biggest structural disadvantage.
Efficacy perception -- HCPs associate efficacy and duration messages more with Beyfortus. Enflonsia needs RWE urgently.
Rep capability -- Enflonsia reps trail across knowledge, credibility, and question-handling metrics. Training investment needed.
Mid-season switching inertia -- Most HCPs won't switch mid-season. Pre-season (July-Sept) is the only window that matters.
Second-season indication gap -- Beyfortus has it; Enflonsia is filing. This matters for high-risk infant coverage.
Cost/access conversations -- HCPs want more clarity on coverage; reps significantly underdeliver on this topic vs. Beyfortus

Strategic Priorities

Win the pre-season window (July-September) with dosing + storage + supply message combination
Close the RWE gap -- Include any available real-world data in materials ASAP
Fix rep performance -- Specifically on addressing HCP questions and providing credible support
Prioritize cost/access conversations -- HCPs are asking; reps aren't delivering
Secure second-season filing -- SMART trial data supports it; filing timing is critical
Target Beyfortus-frustrated practices -- Those who experienced supply issues or dislike weight-based dosing
